Type of medication, because she will randomly divide participants into groups and give the groups different types of medication.

q:

Antianxiety medication is related to reduced depression, because she found a negative correlation between depression levels and the administration of antianxiety medication.

Antidepressants decrease the severity of anxiety, and the experiment demonstrates cause-and-effect because she used random assignment.

(Source 1 )” • Embedded Citation: For example: “According to Source 1 , . . .” Source 1 Introduction In this study, researchers examined the impact of daily gratitude practices on reported happiness among persons with neuromuscular disease. Participants The sample consisted of 65 people ( 44 women, 21 men) with a neuromuscular disorder. Neuromuscular disorders affect the nerves that control voluntary muscles and the nerves that communicate sensory information back to the brain. The participants ranged in age from 22 to 77 years, with a mean age of 49 years. Participants were recruited through a mailing list compiled by the researcher’s institution. Method Participants were randomly assigned one of two conditions: a gratitude condition ( 33 participants) and a control condition ( 32 participants). Both groups completed daily ratings of their subjective well-being. However, participants in the gratitude condition were also asked to respond to the following prompt: There are many things in our lives, both large and small, that we might be grateful about. Think back over the past day and write down up to five things in your life that you are grateful or thankful for. Participants were provided with a packet of 21 daily experience rating forms and envelopes for mailing their forms directly back to the researchers. They were instructed to fill out the form as close to the end of the day as possible. They were told that their ratings were meant to summarize the day as a whole. The daily form took approximately 5 minutes to complete each evening. Participants were asked to mail in their forms once a week and were paid $ 20 if they completed all the forms. The “daily experience rating forms” measured subjective well-being in three ways: Participants were asked to rate how they felt about their life as a whole on a - 3 to + 3 scale, with - 3 meaning terrible and + 3 meaning delighted. Participants rated their expectations for the upcoming week, also on a - 3 to + 3 scale, with - 3 meaning pessimistic, expect the worst and + 3 labeled optimistic, expect the best. Participants indicated how connected they felt with others (where - 3 = isolated and + 3 = well-connected). Results and Discussion Participants in the gratitude condition reported more satisfaction with their lives as a whole, felt more optimism about the upcoming week, and felt more connected with others than did participants in the control condition. Mean ratings for each measure are reported in the table. Emmons, R. A., & McCullough, M. E. (2003). Counting blessings versus burdens: An experimental investigation of gratitude and subjective well-being in daily life. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 84(2), 377–389. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.84.2.377 Source 2 Introduction In this study, subjective well-being was measured in a sample of several thousand middle-aged twin pairs. Comparing levels of similarity between identical and fraternal twins in the sample allowed researchers to investigate the intent to which genetic factors influence happiness. Participants Participants were 2 , 310 members of a large twin registry who were born between 1936 and 1955 . The twins provide a representative sample of the population of Minnesota, which was predominantly White at the time (fewer than 2 % of Minnesota births were to African or Native Americans during this period). Their socioeconomic levels were representative of Minnesota-born adults. Method A self-rating questionnaire was administered to all participants. One of the questionnaire items read, “Taking the good with the bad, how happy and contented are you on the average now, compared with other people?” The twins were asked to make their ratings on a 5 -point scale: 1 = the lowest of the population, 2 = the lower, 3 = the middle, 4 = the upper, and 5 = the highest. Results and Discussion Most of the participants exhibited considerable contentment, and no substantial differences were found between male and female subjects. As shown in Graph 1 , 86 % of the twins rated themselves in the top 35 % in overall contentment. Graph 1 : Contentment Self-Ratings Researchers found that one twin’s score on a measure of subjective well-being was predictive of the second twin’s score, but only for identical twins. Fraternal twins’ scores did not correlate with each other. This was true both for twin pairs raised together and for twin pairs who had been raised apart. Psychological Science, 7, 186-89. https://www.jstor.org/stable/40062939 Source 3 Introduction This study investigated the impact of performing acts of kindness on reported happiness levels among employees in a corporate workplace environment. Participants Employees were recruited from a large corporation in Madrid, Spain. Of the approximately 1 , 200 employees, 88 ( 72 . 7 % female) participated in the study. Participants’ ages ranged from 22 to 55 , and they worked in a variety of departments across the company. They were given both a prize of university merchandise and a donation to a charitable organization based on enrollment in the study. Method All participants were told that they would be practicing a potentially happiness-boosting activity over a number of weeks. They were informed that a computer would randomly assign them to an activity, that it might change from week to week, and that some would not be assigned any activity for the duration of the study. Participants were instructed to keep their activities confidential and focus only on completing their assignments to the best of their abilities. Participants were then randomly assigned to one of three groups: Givers ( 19 participants), Receivers ( 35 participants) and Controls ( 34 participants). No participants were aware of their group assignment. Thus, Receivers were not aware that Givers had been assigned to do acts of kindness on their behalf. Givers were instructed to perform five acts of kindness in one day for recipients on a specific list. They were allowed to choose the specific kinds of activities they did, when they performed them, and whom they chose from their randomized lists of Receivers. Some examples were provided to help them select activities, such as “bringing someone a beverage” or “emailing a thank-you note.” This process was repeated each week for the four weeks of the study, and each Giver was provided a new list of Receivers to select from during each week. Neither Receivers nor Controls performed any other activity assignments throughout the study. All participants logged into the study website every week for 4 weeks to complete surveys that measured happiness and life satisfaction, depression symptoms, and job satisfaction. In a follow-up measure, participants repeated these surveys one month after the acts of kindness assignments had been completed. Results and Discussion Results indicated that both Givers and Receivers benefited from the intervention. At the one-month follow-up, Givers showed statistically significant increases in life satisfaction and job satisfaction and decreases in depressive symptoms. Increases in happiness measures were also observed in Givers, though they were not statistically significant. Receivers showed increases in happiness that were statistically significant. A full month after Givers finished practicing their acts of kindness, the long-term benefits remained, and they were strongest for Givers themselves. The study’s results indicate that acts of kindness produce improvements in well-being for both Givers and Receivers, though Givers benefit more. Results are depicted in the graphs. Introduction In this study, the researchers compared the effectiveness of two different types of exposure therapy for treating post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). Participants Participants were male active duty soldiers who had experienced a trauma during a prior deployment1and had symptoms that met the DSM criteria for PTSD. Participants were restricted both from engaging in other forms of therapy to treat PTSD and from starting any new medicine for PTSD during the study. The final sample included 162 soldiers who agreed to be in the study and knew they could withdraw from participation at any time. The average age of the participants was 30.26 years. All the participants were men. Race and ethnicity demographics are provided in Table 1. Table 1: Race/Ethnicity2Data for Participants Race/Ethnicity Number of Participants Percent of Participants White 97 59.8% Black/African American 15 9.2% Asian/Pacific Islander 10 6.1% Alaskan Indian/American Native 5 3.0% Hispanic 28 12.3% Other (not otherwise identified) 7 4.3% The study was approved by a committee of professionals to ensure the appropriateness of the study, and all treatments considered factors that might impose a difficulty for participants when using exposure therapies in treating PTSD. Method Participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups: a waitlisted group that was eligible to begin therapy but did not receive therapy during the study; an imaginal exposure therapy group in which participants verbally recalled their trauma with their therapist and then practiced controlled breathing techniques and engaged in cognitive and emotional processing of the trauma; and, a virtual reality exposure therapy group in which participants verbally recalled their trauma while wearing a virtual reality headset. As the participants in this group described the trauma they experienced, the therapist customized the virtual reality to mirror their descriptions. Along with the headset, participants wore headphones that introduced sounds associated with the traumas the soldiers described (e.g., gunfire). Special speakers also produced vibrations under the soldiers’ feet. Finally, smells associated with traumas were introduced into the environment. As they experienced the virtual reality simulation, the participants practiced controlled breathing techniques and engaged in cognitive and emotional processing of the experience with their therapist. The study lasted for five weeks. Participants in the two exposure groups received 10 therapy sessions that lasted 1.5 to 2 hours each. After the five-week study concluded, soldiers in the waitlisted group were provided with therapy. Study data were collected by clinicians who were blind to the participants’ treatment groups. The researchers collected data on the following study measures: PTSD symptoms, using a structured interview that measured the number and severity of PTSD symptoms and a PTSD symptom severity survey with a 17-item checklist that used 5-point Likert scales Levels of depression, using a 21-item depression inventory that used 4-point Likert scales Levels of anxiety, using a 21-item anxiety inventory that also used 4-point Likert scales Higher scores on these scales indicated more, or more severe, PTSD symptoms and higher levels of depression and anxiety, respectively. Measures were taken before treatment, at the midpoint of the treatment (2.5 weeks), and at the end of treatment (5 weeks). Results and Discussion At the beginning of the study, the three groups of participants did not differ significantly from each other in terms of PTSD symptoms and severity, levels of depression, or levels of anxiety. Table 2 presents each group’s mean for the same study measures when the study ended (standard deviations appear in parentheses). All differences in the means are statistically significant. Table 2: Means (Standard Deviations) of Study Measures for All Groups Measure Waitlist Imaginal Virtual Reality PTSD symptom structured interview 68.06 (24.27) 44.28 (33.73) 57.07 (32.32) PTSD symptom severity survey 53.99 (11.77) 40.63 (18.57) 45.57 (15.88) Depression inventory 25.63 (25.63) 17.06 (16.18) 18.50 (12.70) Anxiety inventory 18.83 (11.93) 13.28 (12.11) 17.17 (12.80) In an interview conducted during the last week of therapy, researchers also analyzed whether participants in each therapeutic group experienced reliable and significant change in their symptoms of PTSD. The results are depicted in Table 3. Table 3: Participants’ Results at the Last Session of Treatment Waitlist Imaginal Virtual Reality Type of Change Number of Participants Percentage Number of Participants Percentage Number of Participants Percentage Deteriorated/got worse 0 0.00 0 0.00 1 3.33 No reliable change 37 78.72 11 34.38 12 40.00 Reliable change, but not significantly different 5 10.64 5 15.63 7 23.33 Reliable and significant change 5 10.64 16 50.00 10 33.33 The researchers concluded that therapeutic interventions were more effective in treating PTSD and related symptoms than no therapy. Your response to the question should be provided in six parts: A, B, C, D, E, and F. Write the response to each part of the question in complete sentences. Use appropriate psychological terminology in your response. Introduction Pain-related concerns are a primary reason people seek medical care. Most pain-related concerns are addressed using biological therapies such as psychoactive drugs. A growing interest in finding non-biological approaches to pain management led researchers to explore studies that used group therapy as an alternative approach for people experiencing chronic pain. Participants The researchers combined and analyzed findings from 29 peer-reviewed articles that presented studies on the use of group therapy to treat pain. Collectively, the studies included approximately 4,571 participants with an average age of 53.1 years old. Seventy-eight percent of participants were women, and 22% were men.1 Most of the studies did not include other demographic information about the participants, but all of the studies were conducted in English-speaking nations. All of the studies included were approved by a committee of professionals to ensure the appropriateness of the study, and participants agreed to be in the study and knew they could withdraw from participation at any time. The researchers maintained the data in a way that would prevent participant information from being known. Method The researchers included studies that examined group therapy as an effective approach to pain management if those studies met the following conditions: Used random assignment to groups with a manipulated independent variable Were published between 1990 and 2020 Involved participants who were diagnosed with or treated for fibromyalgia2, chronic pain, and/or specific pain Measured changes in pain intensity before and after group therapy that used a variety of psychotherapeutic approaches Used a “treatment-as-usual” or wait-listed control group Included at least 20 participants who completed the group therapy and related assessments Included groups that met at least three times face-to-face for group therapy Most of the studies focused on fibromyalgia or other, nonspecific musculoskeletal pain. Other types of pain reported by participants included chronic back pain, arthritis, combined back and neck pain, cancer-related pain, and headaches or migraines. All of the studies examined the effectiveness of group therapy on pain management. Twelve of the groups had one person lead the sessions, and ten of the groups had more than one person lead the sessions. The most common treatment approach was cognitive-behavioral therapy (17 out of 29 studies). Sessions lasted an average of 120 minutes, with an average of 9 sessions offered per study. Most groups consisted of between 5 and 10 people (reported by 18 of the 29 studies). Results and Discussion The overall effect size of group therapy on reducing pain intensity was small. However, the researchers found significant improvements in patients’ outcomes aside from a reduction in pain intensity, patients reporting decreases in pain interference with their lives, patients thinking their pain is not the worst possible outcome (catastrophizing), and patients’ improved perceptions of pain, anxiety, depression, and barriers to physical functioning. The patients’ improvements remained consistent in long-term follow-ups lasting up to a year. These findings suggest that group therapy helps people who experience chronic pain by lessening their perception of pain intensity rather than alleviating their pain intensity. Researchers also found that group therapy was more effective for women who participated in women-only groups than for women who participated in mixed-gendered groups. According to the researchers, groups that used cognitive-behavioral therapy appeared to be less effective at addressing pain management than groups that used other approaches to therapy (e.g., behavioral therapy). The researchers speculated that since cognitive-behavioral therapy is less focused on building group cohesion, it may not be the best approach for group therapy that addresses pain management. Based on their findings, the researchers concluded that group therapy may be more effective than biological therapies in treating some pain-related concerns. Alldredge, C., Muñiz, V., & Henderson, T.
